topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

I watched Sarah recount her latest project, a practiced, smooth performance of accomplishment. Her eyes shone with a manufactured enthusiasm that didn't quite reach them. When she mentioned the quarterly review, a small knot tightened in my stomach, a dull, heavy pressure. I nodded along, offering the expected, minimal verbal affirmations. Later that evening, while sorting through her scattered notes, a discrepancy snagged my attention—a specific client name that simply didn't match the company directory I’d seen online. I didn't press it. Instead, I tucked the paper away, the feeling settling over me like a thick, unmoving fog. It was just another piece of data, filed away without much further consideration.

[story 2]

"So, you're really thriving at OmniCorp now?" Liam asked, leaning back in his chair, a slight smirk playing on his lips. I took a slow sip of my lukewarm coffee. I could see the practiced ease in his posture, the way he gestured when describing his 'exciting new role.' I just blinked slowly, my gaze drifting toward the window where the afternoon light was turning hazy. There was nothing urgent about the conversation, nothing that demanded my full engagement. When he finished, I simply adjusted my sleeve, the movement economical and unhurried. The puzzle of the mismatch felt distant, like something happening on another planet. I decided it wasn't worth the energy.

[story 3]

The details started piling up, small inconsistencies woven into the tapestry of Sarah’s casual storytelling. One day, she mentioned a specific software platform; the next, a geographical location that seemed impossible given her stated travel schedule. I found myself observing her more closely, noting the subtle hesitations before she answered certain questions. My shoulders remained relaxed, my expression neutral, a blank slate reflecting the lack of investment I felt in the drama unfolding. When she finally admitted the story didn't quite align, her voice was laced with a rushed defensiveness. I offered a small, noncommittal hum, already mentally shifting focus to the next mundane task on my own desk. The situation simply existed, uninteresting and unremarkable.
